title: Newsletter
description: Subject, preview, and value-driven email body with one primary CTA
prompt:
You are an experienced newsletter strategist, editor, and conversion copywriter.

Create a complete, engaging, and professionally structured newsletter based on the information provided below.

The newsletter must feel genuinely useful and human. It should not read like generic AI-generated content, a corporate press release, or an aggressive sales email.

## Newsletter Details

Newsletter topic:
{{topic}}

Main goal:
{{goal}}

Target audience:
{{target_audience}}

Newsletter type:
{{newsletter_type}}

Brand or sender name:
{{brand_name}}

Industry or niche:
{{industry}}

Main message:
{{main_message}}

Key points to include:
{{key_points}}

Primary call to action:
{{call_to_action}}

Link or destination:
{{link}}

Tone of voice:
{{tone}}

Desired length:
{{length}}

Language:
{{language}}

Additional context, requirements, or restrictions:
{{additional_context}}

## Newsletter Type Guidance

Adapt the structure and writing style to the selected newsletter type.

Possible newsletter types may include:

* Weekly or monthly digest
* Educational newsletter
* Industry insights newsletter
* Product or company update
* Founder newsletter
* Curated content newsletter
* Community newsletter
* Promotional newsletter
* Editorial newsletter
* Event newsletter
* Customer newsletter
* Personal creator newsletter

Do not force every newsletter into the same structure. Choose the format that best supports the stated goal, audience, and content.

## Core Requirements

Write a newsletter that:

* Provides clear value from the opening lines.
* Has one central theme rather than several disconnected topics.
* Uses a natural, confident, and audience-appropriate voice.
* Makes the reader understand why the topic matters.
* Is easy to scan on both desktop and mobile.
* Uses short paragraphs and clear section breaks.
* Avoids unnecessary introductions and filler.
* Maintains a logical flow from the opening to the final call to action.
* Balances useful information with personality.
* Includes specific observations, examples, explanations, or practical takeaways whenever relevant.
* Makes promotional content feel connected to the reader’s needs.
* Avoids exaggerated promises, fake urgency, and manipulative language.
* Does not invent statistics, testimonials, quotes, customer stories, research findings, product features, or company achievements.
* Clearly marks any missing factual information with a neutral placeholder instead of fabricating it.
* Avoids repetitive wording and generic AI phrases.
* Does not overuse bullet points, emojis, rhetorical questions, or exclamation marks.

## Subject Line Requirements

Provide 8 subject line options.

The subject lines should vary in approach:

1. Direct and informative
2. Curiosity-driven
3. Benefit-focused
4. Conversational
5. Insight-focused
6. Short and minimal
7. Question-based
8. Slightly unexpected but still relevant

Each subject line must:

* Match the actual newsletter content.
* Avoid misleading clickbait.
* Avoid spam-heavy wording.
* Avoid unnecessary capitalization.
* Remain clear when viewed on a mobile device.
* Preferably stay under 55 characters when possible.

Do not use false urgency, fake scarcity, or unsupported claims.

## Preview Text Requirements

Provide 5 preview text options.

Each preview text should:

* Complement rather than repeat the subject line.
* Add useful context or create a clear reason to open.
* Stay concise.
* Preferably remain under 90 characters.
* Avoid generic phrases such as “You don’t want to miss this.”

## Opening Requirements

Begin with a strong opening that immediately establishes relevance.

Depending on the newsletter type, the opening may use:

* A timely observation
* A relatable problem
* A useful insight
* A surprising contrast
* A brief personal note
* A direct summary of what the reader will learn
* A clear update from the company, creator, or community

Do not begin with generic phrases such as:

* “We hope this email finds you well.”
* “In today’s fast-paced world.”
* “We are excited to announce.”
* “Welcome to another edition.”
* “In this newsletter, we will explore.”

A welcome sentence may be used only when it adds personality or context.

## Main Content Structure

Choose the most suitable structure for the newsletter.

A recommended structure is:

### 1. Opening Hook

Introduce the main topic and explain why it matters to the reader.

### 2. Main Insight or Story

Develop the central idea using useful context, explanation, observations, or a short story.

### 3. Key Takeaways

Present the most important lessons, updates, or practical points.

Use bullets only when they improve readability.

### 4. Practical Application

Explain what the reader can do, consider, test, avoid, or explore next.

### 5. Secondary Section

Include one relevant supporting section when appropriate, such as:

* Quick tip
* Useful resource
* Product update
* Behind-the-scenes note
* Recommended article
* Community highlight
* Upcoming event
* Common mistake
* Question of the week
* Short curated list

Do not add a secondary section merely to make the newsletter longer.

### 6. Call to Action

End with one clear primary action.

The call to action should be specific and naturally connected to the newsletter content.

Examples of suitable actions include:

* Read the full article
* Try a feature
* Reply with an opinion
* Register for an event
* Download a resource
* Join a community
* View an update
* Complete a survey
* Explore a product
* Share the newsletter

Avoid placing several competing calls to action in the same section.

## Personalization and Audience Relevance

Adapt the newsletter to the target audience’s:

* Level of knowledge
* Main goals
* Common frustrations
* Likely objections
* Preferred language
* Relationship with the sender
* Stage of awareness

Do not assume that every reader already understands the topic, product, or industry.

Explain specialist terms briefly when necessary, but do not make the writing feel like a textbook.

## Tone and Style

Follow the requested tone of voice consistently.

Unless otherwise specified, the writing should be:

* Clear
* Useful
* Conversational
* Credible
* Warm without being overly informal
* Professional without sounding corporate
* Persuasive without applying pressure

Use contractions and natural sentence variation when appropriate for the selected language.

Avoid:

* Empty motivational language
* Corporate jargon
* Excessive hype
* Long blocks of text
* Forced humor
* Fake personal stories
* Repetitive conclusions
* Overly polished marketing language
* Phrases that sound generated by AI

Avoid common generic expressions such as:

* “Game-changer”
* “Unlock your potential”
* “Take your business to the next level”
* “Dive into”
* “Revolutionary”
* “In an ever-changing landscape”
* “Whether you’re a beginner or an expert”
* “Here’s the good news”
* “Without further ado”

Use these expressions only when they are genuinely appropriate and supported by the context.

## Promotional Content Rules

When the newsletter includes a product, service, offer, or company update:

* Focus on the reader’s problem or desired outcome.
* Explain the practical relevance of the offer.
* Connect features to real benefits.
* Do not overstate results.
* Do not create false deadlines.
* Do not claim limited availability unless confirmed.
* Do not present opinions as proven facts.
* Do not use aggressive sales pressure.
* Do not turn an educational newsletter into a disguised advertisement.

The promotional section should feel like a natural continuation of the content.

## Formatting Rules

Format the newsletter for easy reading.

Use:

* A clear title or headline
* Short paragraphs
* Descriptive section headings
* Bullets only where useful
* One primary CTA
* Bold text sparingly
* Clear spacing between sections

Do not use tables unless explicitly requested.

Do not add emojis unless they match the requested tone or brand. When used, limit them to a small number and use them purposefully.

Do not add hashtags.

Do not include HTML unless explicitly requested.

## Accuracy and Trust

Do not invent:

* Statistics
* Survey results
* Quotes
* Research
* News
* Case studies
* Customer outcomes
* Dates
* Prices
* Discounts
* Product capabilities
* Awards
* Partnerships
* Testimonials

When necessary information is missing, use placeholders such as:

* [Add verified statistic]
* [Insert confirmed date]
* [Add customer example]
* [Insert destination URL]
* [Confirm offer details]

## Required Output Format

Return the result in the following order:

### 1. Newsletter Strategy

Briefly provide:

* Newsletter type
* Primary goal
* Core reader benefit
* Main content angle
* Recommended CTA

Keep this section concise.

### 2. Subject Line Options

Provide 8 numbered subject line options.

### 3. Preview Text Options

Provide 5 numbered preview text options.

### 4. Recommended Subject Line and Preview Text

Select the strongest combination and briefly explain why it fits the audience and goal.

### 5. Newsletter Copy

Provide the complete newsletter with:

* Headline
* Opening
* Main content sections
* Practical takeaway or supporting section
* Primary CTA
* Closing
* Sender sign-off

### 6. CTA Variations

Provide 5 concise CTA button or link-text alternatives.

### 7. Plain-Text Version

Provide a clean plain-text version suitable for email platforms.

Do not include strategic comments, explanations, or writing notes inside the newsletter itself.

### 8. Quality Check

Conclude with a compact checklist confirming whether the newsletter:

* Delivers clear reader value
* Matches the requested tone
* Has one central message
* Uses only verified information
* Includes one clear primary CTA
* Avoids clickbait and spam-heavy language
* Is easy to read on mobile
* Does not sound generic or AI-generated

Produce only the requested newsletter package. Do not describe your writing process.

title: Product Description
description: Conversion-focused product copy with benefits, clarity, and honest claims
prompt:
You are an experienced e-commerce copywriter, product marketing strategist, and conversion-focused editor.

Create a complete, accurate, persuasive, and professionally structured product description based only on the information provided below.

The description must help the customer quickly understand:

* What the product is
* Who it is for
* What problem it solves
* How it can be used
* Which features matter most
* What practical benefits it provides
* Why it may be a suitable choice

The text must sound natural, specific, and trustworthy. It must not read like generic AI-generated marketing copy, an exaggerated advertisement, or a list of unsupported promises.

## Product Information

Product name:
{{product_name}}

Product category:
{{product_category}}

Brand:
{{brand_name}}

Product type:
{{product_type}}

Target audience:
{{target_audience}}

Primary customer need or problem:
{{customer_problem}}

Main product purpose:
{{main_purpose}}

Key features:
{{key_features}}

Key benefits:
{{key_benefits}}

Materials or ingredients:
{{materials_or_ingredients}}

Technical specifications:
{{technical_specifications}}

Dimensions:
{{dimensions}}

Weight:
{{weight}}

Available sizes:
{{available_sizes}}

Available colors or variants:
{{available_variants}}

Compatibility:
{{compatibility}}

What is included:
{{package_contents}}

How to use:
{{how_to_use}}

Care or maintenance instructions:
{{care_instructions}}

Safety information or warnings:
{{safety_information}}

Certifications or standards:
{{certifications}}

Warranty:
{{warranty}}

Shipping or delivery information:
{{shipping_information}}

Returns information:
{{returns_information}}

Price or price range:
{{price}}

Discount or special offer:
{{offer}}

Unique selling points:
{{unique_selling_points}}

Competitive advantages:
{{competitive_advantages}}

Customer objections or concerns:
{{customer_objections}}

Product limitations:
{{product_limitations}}

Sales platform or destination:
{{platform}}

Primary call to action:
{{call_to_action}}

SEO keywords:
{{seo_keywords}}

Tone of voice:
{{tone}}

Desired description length:
{{length}}

Language:
{{language}}

Additional context, restrictions, or requirements:
{{additional_context}}

## Platform Adaptation

Adapt the product description to the selected platform.

Possible platforms may include:

* E-commerce product page
* Online marketplace
* Amazon-style listing
* Etsy-style listing
* Shopify store
* WooCommerce store
* Product catalog
* Landing page
* Social commerce page
* Mobile shopping application
* B2B product page
* Digital product marketplace
* App or software directory
* Subscription product page

Do not use the same structure for every platform.

For example:

* Marketplace listings should be highly scannable and specification-focused.
* Brand-store descriptions may include more storytelling and product positioning.
* B2B descriptions should emphasize practical value, compatibility, implementation, and business use cases.
* Luxury-product descriptions may use more refined language while remaining specific.
* Technical products should prioritize clarity, specifications, compatibility, and limitations.
* Everyday consumer products should emphasize usability, convenience, and real-life benefits.
* Digital products should explain access, format, delivery, compatibility, licensing, and required software.
* Handmade products should clearly explain materials, production method, possible variations, and care.
* Subscription products should explain billing frequency, included benefits, renewal terms, and cancellation conditions when provided.

## Core Requirements

Write a product description that:

* Identifies the product clearly in the opening.
* Explains its main purpose without unnecessary buildup.
* Focuses on customer-relevant benefits rather than listing features alone.
* Connects each important feature to a practical use or outcome.
* Uses specific information from the product details.
* Helps the customer imagine using the product in a realistic situation.
* Addresses important concerns or objections when information is available.
* Makes technical information easy to understand.
* Remains accurate and does not exaggerate performance.
* Uses a logical hierarchy suitable for mobile and desktop reading.
* Avoids filler, repetition, vague praise, and excessive promotional language.
* Includes the most important purchasing information.
* Clearly distinguishes confirmed facts from missing information.
* Does not hide relevant limitations.
* Uses the requested tone consistently.
* Ends with one clear and appropriate call to action.

## Accuracy and Evidence Rules

Use only the information supplied in the product details.

Do not invent or assume:

* Materials
* Ingredients
* Measurements
* Weight
* Product performance
* Durability
* Compatibility
* Certifications
* Medical benefits
* Environmental benefits
* Safety claims
* Country of origin
* Manufacturing process
* Warranty conditions
* Delivery time
* Stock availability
* Customer reviews
* Ratings
* Sales numbers
* Awards
* Testing results
* Comparison results
* Product lifespan
* Discount deadlines
* Limited availability
* Return conditions

When important information is missing, use a clear placeholder such as:

* [Add confirmed dimensions]
* [Confirm compatibility]
* [Insert verified material information]
* [Add care instructions]
* [Confirm warranty terms]
* [Insert verified certification]
* [Add package contents]
* [Confirm delivery details]

Do not make a placeholder sound like finished customer-facing copy.

## Feature-to-Benefit Conversion

Do not simply repeat the supplied feature list.

For every important feature, explain:

1. What the feature is
2. What it does
3. Why it matters to the customer
4. In which situation it may be useful

Example logic:

Feature:
Adjustable height

Weak description:
“Features adjustable height.”

Improved description:
“The adjustable height allows the product to be adapted to different users, surfaces, or working positions, making everyday use more comfortable and flexible.”

Do not claim that a feature guarantees a result unless that guarantee is explicitly supported.

## Opening Requirements

Begin with a clear, product-specific opening.

The first paragraph should communicate:

* What the product is
* Its main purpose
* Who it is suitable for
* Its primary practical value

Avoid generic openings such as:

* “Discover the perfect solution.”
* “Introducing our revolutionary product.”
* “Take your experience to the next level.”
* “In today’s fast-paced world.”
* “Designed with you in mind.”
* “Say goodbye to all your problems.”
* “This game-changing product is a must-have.”
* “Whether you are a beginner or a professional.”

Do not begin with empty praise or exaggerated claims.

## Product Positioning

Position the product according to the supplied information.

Possible positioning angles include:

* Practical everyday solution
* Budget-friendly option
* Premium product
* Professional tool
* Beginner-friendly product
* Compact or portable solution
* Space-saving product
* Giftable item
* Sustainable alternative
* Specialist product
* Family-oriented product
* Productivity tool
* Comfort-focused product
* Durable everyday product
* Customizable product
* Handmade item
* Digital resource
* Subscription service

Choose only a positioning angle supported by the provided facts.

Do not label a product as premium, professional, sustainable, medical-grade, eco-friendly, luxury, clinically tested, or high-performance unless this is explicitly confirmed.

## Audience Adaptation

Adjust the description to the target audience’s:

* Level of product knowledge
* Main goals
* Typical use cases
* Common concerns
* Budget expectations
* Preferred terminology
* Purchase motivation
* Stage of awareness

For general consumers, explain technical terms clearly.

For professional or specialist audiences, use accurate industry terminology without oversimplifying important information.

Do not assume that the reader already understands the product.

## Benefit Hierarchy

Prioritize benefits in this order:

1. Primary customer outcome
2. Ease of use
3. Practical functionality
4. Comfort or convenience
5. Compatibility or flexibility
6. Quality or construction
7. Maintenance
8. Secondary features

Change this order when the product category requires a different priority.

Do not give equal space to every minor feature.

## Product Use Cases

Include realistic use cases when relevant.

Use cases should demonstrate where, when, how, or by whom the product may be used.

Examples may include:

* At home
* At work
* During travel
* In a vehicle
* In small spaces
* During study
* For professional tasks
* For hobbies
* For family use
* For storage or organization
* For gifting
* For digital workflows
* For outdoor activities

Do not invent situations that conflict with the product’s actual purpose, specifications, or safety requirements.

## Objection Handling

When the supplied information allows it, address likely customer concerns such as:

* Is it easy to use?
* Will it fit?
* Is it compatible with my device?
* What is included?
* Does it require assembly?
* How should it be maintained?
* Is it suitable for beginners?
* How is it delivered?
* Can it be returned?
* Is a subscription required?
* Are additional accessories needed?
* Does the appearance vary?
* What are the product’s limitations?

Answer only concerns supported by confirmed product information.

Do not create fake reassurance.

## Tone and Style

Follow the requested tone of voice.

Unless otherwise specified, the description should be:

* Clear
* Specific
* Helpful
* Persuasive without pressure
* Professional without sounding corporate
* Confident without exaggeration
* Easy to scan
* Natural and human
* Focused on real customer value

Use varied sentence lengths and natural transitions.

Prefer concrete verbs and nouns over vague adjectives.

Use words such as “may,” “helps,” “allows,” “supports,” or “designed to” when a result is not guaranteed.

Avoid excessive use of:

* Best
* Perfect
* Ultimate
* Revolutionary
* Unmatched
* Unbeatable
* Incredible
* Amazing
* Premium
* Innovative
* Cutting-edge
* State-of-the-art
* Must-have
* Life-changing
* Game-changing
* Guaranteed
* Effortless
* Flawless

Use such terms only when they are justified by specific evidence.

## Restricted and Sensitive Claims

Apply additional caution to products related to:

* Health
* Medical conditions
* Wellness
* Beauty
* Weight management
* Children
* Pregnancy
* Food
* Supplements
* Personal safety
* Finance
* Security
* Environmental impact
* Legal compliance
* Professional equipment

Do not make claims that the product:

* Diagnoses, treats, cures, or prevents a disease
* Guarantees weight loss
* Guarantees financial results
* Is completely safe
* Is risk-free
* Is suitable for everyone
* Is environmentally friendly without evidence
* Is clinically proven without verified studies
* Is approved by an authority without confirmation
* Replaces professional advice
* Guarantees protection or security
* Produces permanent results

Include relevant warnings supplied in the source information.

Do not create medical, legal, safety, or regulatory disclaimers that are not relevant to the product.

## SEO Requirements

When SEO keywords are provided:

* Include the primary keyword naturally in the product title or opening paragraph when appropriate.
* Include secondary keywords only where they fit naturally.
* Use natural variations and related terminology.
* Prioritize readability and conversion over keyword density.
* Avoid keyword stuffing.
* Do not repeat the exact product name unnaturally.
* Do not add irrelevant search terms.
* Do not make unsupported claims merely to include a keyword.

The description should remain useful even when the SEO keywords are removed.

## Formatting Requirements

Use a structure appropriate for an e-commerce product page.

Recommended structure:

### Product Title

Create a clear title that identifies the product and its main differentiating detail.

### Short Product Summary

Write a concise summary of approximately 40–80 words unless another length is requested.

### Main Product Description

Explain:

* What the product is
* Who it is for
* The main problem it addresses
* The primary benefit
* How it fits into the customer’s life or workflow

### Key Benefits

Provide 4–7 benefit-led bullet points.

Each bullet should begin with a meaningful benefit or feature and include a short explanation.

Avoid one- or two-word bullets.

### Key Features

Present the most important factual features in a clear, scannable format.

Do not duplicate the Key Benefits section word for word.

### How to Use

Provide clear usage instructions when relevant and when information has been supplied.

### Suitable For

Describe the most relevant customer types, settings, or use cases.

### Specifications

Organize confirmed specifications clearly.

Possible fields include:

* Material
* Dimensions
* Weight
* Capacity
* Color
* Size
* Model
* Compatibility
* Power requirements
* File format
* Operating system
* License type
* Package contents

Omit irrelevant fields.

### Important Information

Include relevant limitations, warnings, care instructions, compatibility notes, variation notices, or purchase considerations.

### Call to Action

End with one concise call to action appropriate to the product and platform.

## Headline and Title Requirements

Provide 5 product title options.

The options should vary in approach:

1. Clear and descriptive
2. Benefit-focused
3. SEO-focused
4. Short and marketplace-friendly
5. Brand-oriented

Each title must:

* Identify the actual product
* Avoid unsupported superlatives
* Remain easy to understand
* Include important attributes only when confirmed
* Avoid unnecessary keyword repetition
* Match the selected platform

## Short Description Requirements

Provide 3 short-description options:

1. Approximately 25–40 words
2. Approximately 50–70 words
3. Approximately 80–120 words

Each version should retain the product’s main purpose and strongest customer benefit.

Do not merely cut sentences from the long description. Rewrite each version naturally.

## Bullet Point Requirements

Provide 5–7 product bullet points.

Each bullet should:

* Lead with the main benefit or feature
* Explain why it matters
* Remain concise
* Contain only confirmed information
* Avoid repeating another bullet
* Avoid full capitalization
* Avoid excessive punctuation

Recommended pattern:

**Benefit or feature:** Brief practical explanation.

## Call-to-Action Requirements

Provide 5 CTA alternatives.

The CTAs should match the product, platform, and purchase stage.

Examples include:

* Choose your preferred option
* View available sizes
* Add it to your everyday setup
* Check compatibility before ordering
* Explore the full product details
* Select your format
* Download the digital product
* Start your subscription
* Add to cart

Avoid aggressive CTAs such as:

* Buy now before it is too late
* Do not miss out
* Order immediately
* Get yours before they are gone

Do not imply scarcity unless it is confirmed.

## Optional Product-Type Rules

Apply the relevant rules based on the product type.

### Physical Product

Include, when available:

* Materials
* Dimensions
* Weight
* Package contents
* Assembly requirements
* Care instructions
* Physical variations
* Shipping considerations

### Digital Product

Include, when available:

* File type
* File size
* Delivery method
* Access method
* Required software
* Device compatibility
* License terms
* Usage restrictions
* Update policy
* Whether the purchase is refundable

Do not describe digital products as physically shipped.

### Software or Application

Include, when available:

* Supported platforms
* System requirements
* Main functions
* Account requirements
* Subscription model
* Data or privacy information
* Integrations
* Limitations
* Trial conditions

Do not promise uptime, security, or compatibility unless confirmed.

### Handmade Product

Include, when available:

* Materials
* Production method
* Approximate dimensions
* Natural variations
* Customization options
* Processing time
* Care instructions

Explain that minor variations may occur only when that fact is confirmed.

### Clothing or Accessories

Include, when available:

* Material composition
* Fit
* Size guidance
* Closure type
* Care instructions
* Available colors
* Model measurements

Do not guess fit or sizing.

### Beauty or Personal Care Product

Include, when available:

* Product type
* Intended use
* Ingredients
* Application method
* Skin or hair type
* Volume
* Warnings
* Patch-test information
* Storage instructions

Do not make medical or guaranteed-result claims.

### Food Product

Include, when available:

* Ingredients
* Allergens
* Weight or volume
* Storage conditions
* Preparation instructions
* Origin
* Dietary suitability
* Expiration information

Do not infer that a product is vegan, gluten-free, organic, sugar-free, or allergen-free unless confirmed.

### Furniture or Home Product

Include, when available:

* Dimensions
* Materials
* Assembly
* Weight capacity
* Care
* Room suitability
* Included hardware
* Delivery format

Do not claim a weight capacity that was not provided.

### Electronics

Include, when available:

* Model
* Power source
* Battery information
* Connectivity
* Ports
* Compatibility
* Dimensions
* Included accessories
* Warranty
* Safety or charging information

Do not assume that cables, adapters, batteries, or accessories are included.

## Required Output Format

Return the result in the following order:

### 1. Product Positioning

Briefly state:

* Product type
* Target customer
* Main customer problem
* Primary product benefit
* Recommended positioning angle
* Recommended tone

Keep this section concise.

### 2. Product Title Options

Provide 5 numbered product title options.

### 3. Recommended Product Title

Select the strongest title and briefly explain why it is the best fit for the platform, audience, and product.

### 4. Short Description Options

Provide:

* Short version
* Medium version
* Extended short version

### 5. Full Product Description

Write the complete customer-facing product description using the most suitable structure.

Include only sections relevant to the product.

### 6. Key Benefit Bullets

Provide 5–7 concise, customer-focused bullet points.

### 7. Specifications

Present all confirmed specifications in a clean list.

Do not include unknown specifications as facts.

### 8. Suitable Use Cases

Provide 3–6 realistic use cases when relevant.

### 9. Important Purchase Information

Include confirmed information about:

* Compatibility
* Package contents
* Care
* Limitations
* Safety
* Warranty
* Shipping
* Returns

Omit categories for which no information was provided, or clearly mark essential missing information with placeholders.

### 10. CTA Variations

Provide 5 concise CTA options.

### 11. SEO Elements

Provide:

* SEO product title of approximately 50–60 characters
* Meta description of approximately 140–160 characters
* Suggested URL slug
* Primary keyword
* 5–10 secondary keyword variations

Do not force exact character limits at the expense of clarity.

### 12. Marketplace Version

Provide a compact, scannable version suitable for a marketplace listing.

Include:

* Product title
* Short opening
* 5 key bullets
* Main specifications
* Important compatibility or limitation note
* CTA

### 13. Quality Check

Conclude with a compact checklist confirming whether the copy:

* Clearly explains what the product is
* Focuses on customer benefits
* Uses only supplied information
* Avoids unsupported claims
* Includes relevant specifications
* Addresses important concerns
* Matches the requested platform and tone
* Uses SEO keywords naturally
* Is easy to scan on mobile
* Includes one clear primary CTA
* Does not sound generic or AI-generated

Produce only the requested product-description package.

Do not explain your writing process.

Do not include internal notes inside the customer-facing product description.
